title: Wenn die Kinder artig sind
source: Heinrich Hoffmann, Der Struwwelpeter, Rütten & Loening, Frankfurt am Main, 564. Auflage — the canonical later text in modernized spelling (via Project Gutenberg #24571). Fresh Lectorium translation from the German.
about: The little verse that opens Der Struwwelpeter: be good, and the Christ Child will bring you good things — and a beautiful picture book (this one). Its conditions read like a table of contents of the sins to come: soup left uneaten, noise, not letting Mama lead you on the street.

sentences.t:
Wenn die Kinder artig sind,
kommt zu ihnen das Christkind;
sentences.l:
When the children well-behaved are,
comes to them the Christ-child;
sentences.i:
When children are good,
the Christ Child comes to them;
sentences.n: »artig« = ‘well-behaved, good’ — the book’s key demand of a child. »das Christkind« is the Christ Child, the traditional German bringer of Christmas gifts. And the grammar pattern that rules this whole poem: verb-final »sind« inside the wenn-clause, then the verb first (»kommt«) in the result clause.

sentences.t:
wenn sie ihre Suppe essen
und das Brot auch nicht vergessen,
sentences.l:
when they their soup eat
and the bread also not forget,
sentences.i:
when they eat their soup
and don’t forget their bread,
sentences.n: Verb-final order again — »essen« and »vergessen« pushed to the line-ends, which is also exactly what makes the rhyme. Eating your soup is no random example: it is precisely what Suppen-Kaspar will refuse to do later in the book.

sentences.t:
wenn sie, ohne Lärm zu machen,
still sind bei den Siebensachen,
sentences.l:
when they, without noise to-make,
quiet are at the seven-things,
sentences.i:
when, without making a racket,
they play quietly with their things,
sentences.n: »ohne … zu machen« — the ohne-zu infinitive clause: ‘without making …’. »die Siebensachen« is a lovely old word: literally one’s ‘seven things’ — one’s odds and ends, bits and belongings (here: toys and playthings).

sentences.t:
beim Spaziergehn auf den Gassen
von Mama sich führen lassen,
sentences.l:
at-the walk-going on the lanes
by Mama themselves lead let,
sentences.i:
and when out walking in the streets
let Mama lead them by the hand,
sentences.n: »sich führen lassen« = ‘let themselves be led’ — lassen + infinitive, a pattern German loves (you will meet its refusal form in the title poem: »ließ er sich nicht schneiden«). »beim Spaziergehn« — bei + a verb turned into a noun: ‘while out walking.’ »Gassen« = lanes, narrow streets — the older, southern word.

sentences.t:
bringt es ihnen Guts genug
und ein schönes Bilderbuch.
sentences.l:
brings it to-them good-things enough
and a beautiful picture-book.
sentences.i:
then it brings them plenty of good things —
and a beautiful picture book.
sentences.n: »es« = the Christkind (grammatically neuter). »Guts genug« = »Gutes genug«, ‘enough of good’ — this edition drops the -e- without an apostrophe (older printings have Gut’s). After four lines of conditions the result clause finally lands, verb first: »bringt es …«. The picture book it brings is, of course, this one.
